narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 49% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your profile: you ranked in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's mid-range performance—along with a low family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the average of 3.21), an LSAT score of 40 (slightly above the average of 36.7), and being an Asian male planning full-time studies. While most applicants are full-time like you and males are slightly more common, your racial background is less represented than the dominant White majority in the dataset. The model's decision was driven primarily by your low first-year decile rank, which had the strongest influence in pushing the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which surprisingly also nudged the outlook toward failure in this context, followed closely by your low third-year decile. Your low family income then played a notable role in the same direction, with your racial background contributing further, and your undergraduate GPA adding to the risk signal. Less influential but still pushing toward failure were your plans for full-time enrollment and your gender. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and later school deciles stand out as particularly weak, while your LSAT offers a relative bright spot but wasn't enough to offset the rest. To improve your future chances, focus on realistic steps like building stronger academic habits to boost any upcoming grades or equivalent performance metrics, as low deciles were the top concerns. You might also consider targeted LSAT prep or retakes if possible, since it ranked highly despite your solid score. Addressing study skills or seeking support for challenges tied to family income could help simulate better outcomes. With targeted improvements in these academic areas, you could shift the prediction in your favor next time. Keep pushing forward—you have strengths to build on.
